aspects.problem_formulation: State machine replication (SMR) traditionally requires servers to store the full history of committed commands and relies on leader-based or Byzantine-tolerant protocols that fail under an adaptive adversary capable of blocking arbitrary servers. The paper addresses how to achieve scalable, lightweight, leaderless SMR with safety, liveness, and fast recovery under a 1-late blocking adversary, while offloading history-keeping to clients via signature-free certificates.
aspects.proposed_method: The authors adapt the median rule from stabilizing consensus, combined with undecided-state dynamics, to a client-server SMR setting using gossip-based push/pull communication. They extend it to a (k, ℓ)-median rule (specifically k=6, ℓ=3) over logs, then to a compact variant that lets servers commit and forget commands while clients hold small Merkle-hash-forest certificates; a checkpoint+reset recovery protocol handles arbitrary blocking. The protocol is fully decentralized, signature-free (only collision-resistant hashing), and provides logarithmic latency, communication, and storage per command.
aspects.experimental_results: This is a theoretical paper with no empirical experiments; the headline result is a leaderless gossip-based SMR protocol that guarantees liveness against any 1-late, 1/10-blocking adversary, strong safety against any 1-late adversary regardless of the number of blocked servers, and logarithmic-time recovery, while achieving logarithmic communication, latency, and per-server/per-client storage — tolerating Ω(n) blocking failures versus Avalanche's O(√n).

extras.paper_type: theoretical / protocol design with formal proofs
core_techniques: Merkle hash forest / Merkle Mountain Range

aspects.problem_formulation: The paper addresses Atomic Broadcast — collectively constructing a total ordering of user-submitted transactions among N nodes in an asynchronous network where up to f = (N-1)/3 nodes are Byzantine — with the goal of improving over HoneyBadgerBFT's logarithmic latency and removing its trusted-dealer assumption while preserving optimal communication complexity.
aspects.proposed_method: The authors present Aleph, an ABFT atomic broadcast protocol whose network layer maintains a common Communication History DAG (ch-DAG) of signed 'units' grown via reliable broadcast, cleanly separating networking from protocol logic so that ordering decisions are combinatorial properties of the local DAG. Aleph achieves O(1) expected latency and matches optimal communication complexity, and is paired with a novel trustless ABFT Randomness Beacon (Setup + Toss) that replaces the trusted dealer used by prior schemes.
experimental_baselines: Validated Asynchronous Byzantine Agreement (Abraham et al.)
aspects.experimental_results: This is a theoretical paper: Aleph is proven to implement Atomic Broadcast over N=3f+1 nodes with expected O(N/k) round latency for transactions input to k honest nodes (i.e., O(1) under the paper's transaction-buffer assumption), O(T + R·N^2 log N) per-node communication over R rounds, a 3-round optimistic-case validation latency, and an ABFT Randomness Beacon whose Setup runs in O(1) rounds with O(N^2 log N) communication and each Toss in 1 round with O(N) communication.

extras.venue: AFT (Advances in Financial Technologies), referenced as the Aleph Zero foundational paper
extras.system_model: Asynchronous network, Byzantine faults, N = 3f + 1, unforgeable signatures, point-to-point messaging
extras.attack_analysis: Introduces the 'Fork Bomb' spam attack against DAG-based protocols and discusses Aleph's resistance via reliable broadcast

aspects.problem_formulation: Overlay networks rely on membership management mechanisms that, when targeted by Byzantine attackers, can compromise the entire overlay (e.g., via false crash reports, false liveness reports, or eclipse attacks); existing approaches struggle to provide secure, scalable membership maintenance that resists such attacks while supporting moderate churn.
aspects.proposed_method: Fireflies is an overlay protocol that organizes members into a verifiable pseudorandom mesh of k membership rings derived from a secure hash of member and ring identifiers, assigning each member k pseudorandom monitors. Members issue signed notes, accusations, and rebuttals validated by a trusted CA, gossip them over a secure channel, and use a bitmask in notes to disable up to t corrupt monitors (with k=2t+1) so that at least one correct monitor remains, while Chernoff bounds show k=Θ(log N) suffices to keep unfortunate nodes (majority-corrupt neighborhoods) constant in expectation.
aspects.experimental_results: Evaluation via simulations and a PlanetLab deployment demonstrates that Fireflies is a practical approach for secure membership maintenance in overlay networks with moderate churn, providing each correct member with a full membership view and a low-diameter, connected gossip subgraph with high probability.

extras.venue: ACM Transactions on Computer Systems (TOCS), Vol. 33, No. 2, Article 5, May 2015
extras.paper_type: journal article (extended version of Johansen et al. 2006)
attack_model: bounded fraction p_corrupt of live members are corrupt
trusted CA oracle for identity assignment
key_parameters: Δ probabilistic upper bound on gossip latency
